questions.question: What does 'scroll-behavior: smooth' do?
questions.explanation: scroll-behavior: smooth enables animated scrolling for navigational actions like anchor links and scrollIntoView(). It does NOT affect user-initiated scrolling.

questions.question: Which element should you apply scroll-behavior to for page-level smooth scrolling?
questions.explanation: Apply scroll-behavior: smooth to the html element for page-level smooth scrolling. Some browsers require the property on html specifically to work correctly.

questions.question: Which type of scrolling is affected by scroll-behavior: smooth?
questions.explanation: Only navigational scrolling is affected — anchor links, scrollIntoView(), scrollTo(), and scrollBy(). User-initiated scrolling (wheel, touch, scrollbar) is not affected.

questions.question: Can you customize the duration or easing of the smooth scroll animation?
options: No, the browser controls timing for consistency and performance
Only in Chrome, using experimental flags
Yes, using JavaScript to override the CSS property
questions.explanation: The browser controls the duration and timing of smooth scroll animations. There is no CSS API to customize these parameters.

questions.question: What CSS property should you combine with smooth scrolling to prevent fixed headers from covering anchor targets?
questions.explanation: scroll-margin-top on the target section adds a margin that offsets the scroll position, preventing the fixed header from covering the section.

questions.question: Which media query should you use to disable smooth scrolling for users with motion sensitivity?
questions.explanation: prefers-reduced-motion: reduce targets users who have requested minimal motion in their system settings. Override scroll-behavior to auto for these users.

questions.question: What JavaScript method triggers smooth scrolling when scroll-behavior: smooth is set?
questions.explanation: scrollIntoView() respects the CSS scroll-behavior property. You can also specify behavior: 'smooth' in the options parameter for programmatic control.

questions.question: In which Safari version was scroll-behavior support added?
questions.explanation: Safari added scroll-behavior support in version 15.4 (2022). This was the last major browser to implement the property.

questions.question: What event fires when a smooth scroll animation completes?
questions.explanation: The scrollend event fires when a scroll animation completes, whether it was smooth or instant.

questions.question: Does scroll-behavior affect browser Find in Page functionality?
questions.explanation: Whether smooth scrolling affects Find in Page is browser-dependent. Some browsers respect the CSS property, others don't.

questions.question: What is the default value of scroll-behavior?
questions.explanation: The default value is auto, meaning navigational scrolling happens instantly without animation.

questions.question: Can scroll-behavior be applied to any scrollable element, or only the document?
options: Any scrollable element with overflow: auto/scroll
questions.explanation: scroll-behavior can be applied to any scrollable element that has overflow: auto or overflow: scroll, not just the document.

questions.question: What happens when you use scroll-behavior: smooth on an element without overflow?
options: Nothing — scroll-behavior only affects scrollable elements
It creates a smooth animation effect on the element
It applies smooth scrolling to the parent
questions.explanation: scroll-behavior has no effect on non-scrollable elements. The element must have overflow content and overflow: auto or scroll.

questions.question: What is a key performance advantage of CSS smooth scrolling over JavaScript scroll libraries?
options: It runs on the compositor thread, not the main JavaScript thread
It uses requestAnimationFrame for better performance
It's faster because it skips rendering
It uses Web Workers for scroll calculations
questions.explanation: CSS smooth scrolling runs on the browser's compositor thread, separate from the main JavaScript thread, preventing jank and ensuring smooth 60fps animation.

questions.question: How do you programmatically scroll to the top of a page with smooth animation?
questions.explanation: window.scrollTo() with the behavior: 'smooth' option scrolls to coordinates with smooth animation. Setting top: 0 scrolls to the top.

questions.question: Which CSS approach correctly disables all animations for users who prefer reduced motion?
questions.explanation: Setting extremely small durations (0.01ms) effectively disables animations while still allowing the animation end events to fire, preventing bugs in animation-dependent code.

questions.question: What is a use case where scroll-behavior: auto is preferred over smooth?
options: Rapid navigation UI (tabs, accordions) where smooth feels sluggish
Blog posts with table of contents
questions.explanation: For rapid-interaction UI elements like tab panels or accordions, smooth scrolling can feel sluggish and unresponsive. auto provides instant feedback.

questions.question: What CSS feature helps users orient themselves after smooth scrolling to an anchor?
options: CSS Transitions on the body element
questions.explanation: The :target pseudo-class applies styles to the element whose ID matches the URL hash. Adding a highlight animation to :target helps users locate the scrolled-to section.

questions.question: Which browsers support scroll-behavior?
questions.explanation: Chrome 61+ (2017), Firefox 60+ (2018), Safari 15.4+ (2022), and Edge 79+ (2020) support scroll-behavior. IE does not support it.

questions.question: What is the relationship between scroll-behavior and CSS Scroll Snap?
options: They are complementary — scroll-behavior controls the animation, scroll snap controls the landing position
They are mutually exclusive — using both causes conflicts
Scroll-behavior is a subset of scroll snap
questions.explanation: scroll-behavior controls HOW the scroll animates (smooth vs instant), while scroll snap controls WHERE the scroll lands (snap positions). They work together perfectly.
